title: After ANOVA: which groups actually differ
intro: ANOVA only says "there is a difference somewhere". But the business needs "WHICH group is better". The naive answer — run t-tests for all pairs — leads straight into the multiple comparisons trap. That is why special post-hoc procedures follow ANOVA.

beats.text: ANOVA said "there is a difference somewhere". But between WHICH of the three groups? The chart shows confidence intervals of the pairwise differences (A−B, A−C, B−C). The rule is simple: if an interval does not cover zero (green) — the difference is significant; if it covers it (gray) — it is not. Move the between-group and within-group spreads — the intervals respond.
The temptation is to compare all pairs with t-tests.
beats.predict: With 4 groups that is 6 pairs. If each is tested at the 5% level, what happens to the chance of at least one false finding?
beats.reveal: It jumps to about 1 − 0.95⁶ ≈ 26% — every fourth "analysis" catches a nonexistent difference. This is exactly the multiple comparisons trap: the more pairs, the more random "wins". That is why uncorrected pairwise t-tests after ANOVA are a mistake.

beats.text: The solution — post-hoc procedures with a multiplicity correction. The most famous is Tukey's test (HSD): it compares all pairs while holding the OVERALL false-positive risk at 5%, automatically tightening the threshold to the number of comparisons. Switch the "Tukey correction" toggle off on the chart: the intervals narrow and more pairs become "significant" — that is precisely the inflation of false findings under naive pairwise tests. Simpler corrections exist too (Bonferroni divides α by the number of pairs).

The logic is two-step: first ANOVA as the "gate" (is there any difference at all), and only if it is significant — the post-hoc (where exactly). This preserves power: we don't run a pile of corrected comparisons until we're sure the signal exists. A/B/C/D tests with several variants work the same way.

The link to the course: this is a direct continuation of the multiple comparisons lesson, in the context of comparing groups. And a bridge to regression: ANOVA with a post-hoc is equivalent to a regression on a categorical feature with pairwise contrasts — the same "signal against noise" idea under different names.

definitions.text: comparisons after a significant ANOVA to find out which groups actually differ.
a post-hoc procedure for all pairs that holds the overall false-positive risk at the chosen level.
the probability of at least one false finding among all comparisons; this is what the corrections control.
first ANOVA (is there a difference), then the post-hoc (where exactly) — preserves power.

practice: The proper multi-group analysis: ANOVA → if significant → a corrected post-hoc (Tukey). That way you neither drown in pairwise tests nor miss which group truly stands out.
The same in A/B/C/D tests: first check whether the variants differ at all, then corrected comparisons against the control. Otherwise, with many variants the "winner" is often just chance.
realLife: Agronomy and medicine are the birthplace of post-hocs: compare several fertilizers or doses and figure out which one is actually better without harvesting false differences.
Product work: comparing several landing page versions or pricing grids — ANOVA/regression at the gate, then corrected pairwise comparisons to pick the variant.
assumptions: Post-hocs inherit ANOVA's assumptions (roughly normal residuals, similar group spread, independence). When violated — nonparametric analogues (Kruskal–Wallis + Dunn).
Controlling the family-wise error reduces the power of each individual comparison: the honest price for the right to test many pairs at once.
